stem: A nurse is caring for a 68-year-old male with pneumonia receiving mechanical ventilation. The ventilator's high-pressure alarm activates, and the nurse notes restlessness and diminished breath sounds on the left side. Prioritize the nursing actions from first to last.

options.hoverRationale: This is the priority assessment to confirm the location and nature of the respiratory issue, such as a mucus plug or tube displacement, which is causing the high-pressure alarm.
After assessment confirms potential obstruction, suctioning is the immediate intervention to clear secretions, which are a common cause of high-pressure alarms and can lead to hypoxemia.

If suctioning fails to resolve the alarm, assessing tube depth is critical. Unilateral diminished sounds suggest potential displacement into the right mainstem bronchus, a life-threatening complication.
Collaboration is vital, but only after performing initial assessments and interventions. The nurse must provide the RT with specific findings to facilitate effective troubleshooting and management.
This diagnostic test confirms tube placement and rules out other causes like pneumothorax. However, it is performed after immediate life-saving assessments and interventions have been attempted.

itemContext.sbar: S: Robert Jones is a 68-year-old male admitted for pneumonia and currently intubated and mechanically ventilated. His current respiratory rate is 24, FiO2 is 40%, PEEP is 5 cm H2O, and Tidal Volume is 450 mL. A: He has a history of COPD and hypertension. At 07:00, his ABG results were: pH 7.30, PaCO2 55 mmHg, PaO2 85 mmHg, HCO3 26 mEq/L, and SpO2 94%. At 07:15, the high-pressure alarm activated on the ventilator. B: Auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds on the left side. His BP is 160/90 mmHg, and his HR is 110 bpm. O: The patient is restless and appears uncomfortable. The endotracheal tube is secured at 22 cm at the lip. A chest X-ray order has been placed, and the image will be available in the EHR shortly. I need you to immediately evaluate the patient, focusing on the cause of the high-pressure alarm and diminished breath sounds. Please adjust ventilator settings as needed, in collaboration with respiratory therapy, to improve ventilation and oxygenation based on your assessment findings.

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content: ABG (07:00): pH 7.30, PaCO2 55 mmHg, PaO2 85 mmHg, HCO3 26 mEq/L, SpO2 94%. CBC: WBC 15,000/mm3, Hgb 14 g/dL, Hct 42%, Platelets 250,000/mm3. Electrolytes: Na 140 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, HCO3 26 mEq/L.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Ceftriaxone 1g IV q12h (scheduled). Albuterol/Ipratropium nebulizer q4h PRN wheezing (last dose 06:00). Famotidine 20mg IV q12h (scheduled). Propofol infusion 20 mcg/kg/min (continuous).

tabs.title: Vitals
tabs.content: 06:00: BP 150/85 mmHg, HR 98 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 95%. 07:00: BP 160/90 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 24, SpO2 94%. Ventilator Settings: FiO2 40%, PEEP 5 cm H2O, Tidal Volume 450 mL, Mode: Assist Control.

clinicalPearls: High-pressure ventilator alarms often indicate airway obstruction (e.g., secretions, kinked tubing) or decreased lung compliance (e.g., pneumothorax, ARDS).
Unilateral diminished breath sounds in an intubated patient strongly suggest endotracheal tube displacement into a mainstem bronchus, most commonly the right.

Always follow the nursing process: assess the patient before intervening. In ventilator emergencies, a rapid bedside assessment is critical before involving other team members or ordering diagnostics.

questionTrap.trap: Prioritizing a diagnostic test like a chest X-ray or immediately calling for help before performing a basic nursing assessment and intervention. While these are important steps, they should not precede a rapid bedside evaluation.
questionTrap.howToOvercome: Remember the DOPE mnemonic for ventilator alarms (Displacement, Obstruction, Pneumothorax, Equipment failure). This framework guides the nurse to perform rapid bedside checks (auscultation, tube position, suctioning) before escalating care.

mnemonic.title: DOPE
mnemonic.expansion: Displacement, Obstruction, Pneumothorax, Equipment failure. (Common causes of respiratory distress in intubated patients)
